coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheets modified with N-doped carbon dots: Enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production activity and mechanism insight
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Shang, Yaru
Liu, Tianxing
Chen, Gang
Wang, Yu
coredata.dc:description: In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) modified ultrathin carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanosheets were synthesized by a simple secondary calcination method, which realizes efficient photocatalytic hydrogen production. The secondary calcination process can not only thermally exfoliate g-C3N4 into ultrathin nanosheets, but also uniformly anchor NCDs (∼3 nm) on the surface of g-C3N4. The photocatalytic results manifested that a very small amount of NCDs (0.5 wt%) loading on g-C3N4 could achieve a high photocatalytic hydrogen production rate (5.21 mmol g−1 h−1). The reasons for the enhanced photocatalytic activity were analyzed. The ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheets facilitated the migration of photogenerated carriers. Moreover, NCDs/g-C3N4 exhibited enhanced visible light absorption owing to the up-conversion fluorescence effect of NCDs and the narrow band gap caused by the decoration of NCDs. Furthermore, NCDs as electron acceptors can promote carrier separation. This combination strategy provides a basis for the synthesis of inexpensive, metal-free, and efficient carbon-based photocatalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: g-C3N4 nanosheets
N-doped carbon dots
Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution
Upconversion
Carrier separation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a,b) TEM and HRTEM images of NCDs. (c) The size distribution and (d) FTIR spectrum of NCDs.
XPS spectra of NCDs: (a) Survey; (b) C 1s; (c) N 1s; (d) O 1s. (e) UV–vis absorption spectra and (f) up-converted PL spectra of NCDs.
SEM images of (a) CN and (b) NCDs(0.5)/CN. (c) TEM image of NCDs(0.5)/CN. (d) AFM image and (e,f) corresponding height profiles of NCDs(0.5)/CN. (g–i) TEM and HRTEM images of NCDs(0.5)/CN.
(a) XRD patterns and (b) FTIR spectra. XPS spectra of CN and NCDs(0.5)/CN: (c) C 1s, (d) N 1s.
(a) UV–vis DRS spectra. (b) (αhν)2 versus hν curves and (c) XPS valence band spectra. (d) Schematic diagram of band structures.
(a) PL spectra. (b) Fitting time-resolved PL spectra. (c) Photocurrent response curves. (d) EIS curves.
(a) Photocatalytic H2 evolution curves and (b) the average H2 production rate.

(a) UV–visible absorption spectrum and the wavelength-dependent AQE of NCDs(0.5)/CN. (b) Photocatalytic H2 production cycle test of NCDs(0.5)/CN. (c) PL spectra and (d) FTIR spectra for fresh and used NCDs(0.5)/CN.
Schematic diagram of possible photocatalytic mechanism in NCDs/CN composite.
Schematic diagram of the synthesis process of NCDs/CN.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheets modified with N-doped carbon dots: Enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production activity and mechanism insight

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MIIT Key Laboratory of Critical Materials Technology for New Energy Conversion and Storage, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, 150001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MIIT Key Laboratory of Critical Materials Technology for New Energy Conversion and Storage
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: MIIT Key Laboratory of Critical Materials Technology for New Energy Conversion and Storage, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, 150001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) modified ultrathin carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanosheets were synthesized by a simple secondary calcination method, which realizes efficient photocatalytic hydrogen production. The secondary calcination process can not only thermally exfoliate g-C3N4 into ultrathin nanosheets, but also uniformly anchor NCDs (∼3 nm) on the surface of g-C3N4. The photocatalytic results manifested that a very small amount of NCDs (0.5 wt%) loading on g-C3N4 could achieve a high photocatalytic hydrogen production rate (5.21 mmol g−1 h−1). The reasons for the enhanced photocatalytic activity were analyzed. The ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheets facilitated the migration of photogenerated carriers. Moreover, NCDs/g-C3N4 exhibited enhanced visible light absorption owing to the up-conversion fluorescence effect of NCDs and the narrow band gap caused by the decoration of NCDs. Furthermore, NCDs as electron acceptors can promote carrier separation. This combination strategy provides a basis for the synthesis of inexpensive, metal-free, and efficient carbon-based photocatalysts.

Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) modified ultrathin carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanosheets were synthesized. The enhanced photocatalytic mechanism of NCDs/g-C3N4 composites was analyzed.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen (H2) energy is an efficient, green and sustainable energy source. Photocatalytic water splitting to produce hydrogen by solar energy is one of the most promising technologies to solve the energy crisis and environmental pollution problems [1,2]. Various photocatalysts with suitable energy band structures, including metal sulfides [3], metal oxides [4], and metal phosphides [5], have been demonstrated to possess the ability of photocatalytic hydrogen production. However, poor visible light response and thermochemical stability limit their applications. Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), a metal-free polymer semiconductor, has become an ideal catalyst in the field of photocatalytic H2 evolution due to its low cost, suitable energy band, high chemical stability, and visible light response [6,7]. g-C3N4 is composed of a highly delocalized π-conjugated layered structure formed by sp2 hybridization of carbon (C) and nitrogen (N) atoms [8,9]. However, g-C3N4 usually prepared by thermal polycondensation is tightly packed, which restricts the interlayer migration of charge carriers [10]. Moreover, the low surface area and limited light utilization (≤460 nm) of bulk g-C3N4 also constrain its application in the field of photocatalysis [11].

Many strategies have been developed to enhance the application potential of g-C3N4, such as metal element doping, morphology tailoring, construction of heterojunctions, surface modification. The tailoring of nanostructures (nanosheets [12,13], microspheres [14,15], nanotubes [16], etc.) can accelerate the electron transfer rate and improve the surface photocatalytic efficiency [17]. However, metal element doping is mostly related to metal ions, which almost inevitably increase the toxicity of catalysts [11]. The construction of heterojunction photocatalysts requires band structure matching and usually sacrifices their redox abilities. Therefore, it is crucial to search metal-free materials with outstanding catalytic activity and environmental compatibility to modify g-C3N4. Carbon quantum dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional (0D) carbon nanomaterials with an average size of less than 10 nm [18,19]. It has attracted much attention in recent years owing to its unique size, non-toxicity, excellent electronic conductivity and optical properties [20]. The conjugated structure of CDs may enhance the interaction between CDs and semiconductors. Many CDs/semiconductor composites have been discovered successively, such as CDs/TiO2 [21], CDs/Cu2O [22], CDs/BiO1-xBr [23] and so on. According to reports, CDs-modified semiconductors broaden the light absorption range through the up-conversion effect of CDs, and CDs can act as an excellent electron acceptor, contributing to reduce the recombination of electrons and holes [24]. In view of the above advantages, some CDs-modified g-C3N4 has been applied in the field of photocatalysis. Liu et al. reported that CDs surface-sensitized g-C3N4 composites showed enhanced photocatalytic H2 production activity [25]. Jin et al. synthesized CDs-modified ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheets with the capacity for direct ⋅OH and ⋅O2 − yields and a broad-spectrum response [26]. Li et al. prepared a novel tubular g-C3N4 decorated with CDs, which demonstrated better photocatalytic carbamazepine removal activity [16]. The above studies indicate that the coupling of CDs with g-C3N4 is an effective way to develop high-performance g–C3N4–based photocatalysts. However, the preparation process of these CDs/g-C3N4 composites is complicated, and the enhancement mechanism of photocatalysis is still imperfect, so further research is needed to improve the photocatalytic activity. It is well known that the electronegativity of N atoms is higher than that of C atoms, so the introduction of N atoms in CDs can regulate charge redistribution of nearby C atoms. N-doped CDs can exhibit better electron transfer and light absorption ability than CDs [18].

Herein, ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheets were fabricated by a facile secondary thermal exfoliation method, and then a small amount of N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were decorated on g-C3N4 to synthesize NCDs/g-C3N4 composites. AFM image proved that the thickness of g-C3N4 nanosheets (∼8 nm) after secondary calcination belongs to ultrathin nanolayer. The effects of NCDs content on the photocatalytic performance was investigated. The results showed that loading a small amount of NCD on CN could significantly boost the photocatalytic hydrogen production performance. The structure, morphology, and photoelectrochemical properties of NCDs/CN were investigated in detail, and the mechanism of the enhanced photocatalytic activity was speculated.

Folic acid (C19H19N7O6), ethylene glycol (C2H6O2), urea (CH4N2O), and triethanolamine (C6H15NO3, TEOA) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. All materials or chemicals applied were of analytical grade without additional purification.

The NCDs were prepared by hydrothermal method according to literature [20]. Typically, 0.2 g of folic acid, 15 mL of ethylene glycol, and 15 mL of deionized (DI) water were mixed by sonication for 20 min, then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 170 °C for 24 h. After natural cooling, the brown-yellow solution was dialyzed in DI water for 36 h with a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off of 3500 Da). Subsequently, the NCDs were obtained by freeze-drying. CDs were synthesized by the same method and procedure using glucose instead of folic acid.

Firstly, 20 g of urea was placed in a crucible with a cover, heated to 550 °C at a rate of 5 °C·min−1, and calcined in air for 4 h to obtain g-C3N4. As for preparing NCDs/CN photocatalyst, different amounts (0.3, 0.75, 1.5 mg) of NCDs were dispersed in a small amount of water and then mixed with 150 mg of prepared g-C3N4. Afterwards, the mixture was calcined at 650 °C for 2 h (5 °C⋅min−1, Ar atmosphere). The final products were labeled as NCDs(x)/CN (x = 0.2, 0.5, 1), respectively. Without the addition of NCDs, the ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheets were prepared by secondary calcination and named as CN. The schematic diagram of the synthesis process of NCDs/CN was shown in Scheme 1 .

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded by a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation. The morphologies and microstructures of the samples were observed by a SUPRA55 field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM) and a FEI Tecnai G2 F30 transmission electron microscope (TEM). The thickness of the catalyst was imaged using an Asylum Research Cypher-ES atomic force microscope (AFM). The compositions were analyzed using a Fourier transform infrared (FTIR, Nicolet is50) spectrophotometer and Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) with an Al Kα radiation source. The UV–visible (UV–vis) diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were detected on a HITACHI UH-4150 spectrophotometer. Time-resolved transient decay spectra and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were acquired on HORIBA FluoroMax-4. The photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) tests were performed under a standard three-electrode configuration by an AUTOLAB-PGSTAT302 N electrochemical working station. The photocatalyst coated on FTO glass, platinum sheet and saturated Ag/AgCl were used as working electrode, counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively.

The photocatalytic H2 evolution experiment was performed in a vacuum closed circulation system, in which 10 mg of as-prepared photocatalyst was dispersed in a mixed solution (45 mL of water, 5 mL of TEOA and a certain amount of H2PtCl6 (1 wt% Pt)). After irradiation with a 300 W Xe lamp (λ ≥ 420 nm), the generated H2 was detected by gas chromatograph (GC-7920).

The synthesis process of NCDs/CN was shown in Scheme 1. The NCDs prepared by the hydrothermal method exhibit uniformly dispersed spherical structure (Fig. 1 a and b). The size of these NCDs is mainly concentrated in 1–4 nm (Fig. 1c). The HRTEM image and its inset clarify the typical hexagonal graphitic crystal structure of NCDs (Fig. 1b). Its crystal plane spacing is 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of NCDs [27,28]. In the FTIR spectra of NCDs (Fig. 1d), the peaks in the range of 3000–3500 cm−1 represent –OH and N–H bonds [29]. The peaks at approximately 765 cm−1, 1417 cm−1, 1523 cm−1, and 1700 cm−1 correspond to C–O, C–N, –NH, and CO chemical bonds, respectively [30]. The band at 1611 cm−1 is associated with the stretching modes of CC bond, which is derived from the photoluminescence of NCDs caused by π-π∗ transition [31]. These results illustrate that there are some functional groups on the surface of NCDs, such as hydroxyl group, carboxyl group, imino groups, etc., their existence can improve the solubility and electron transfer ability of NCDs [18].

XPS spectra were tested to further analyze the chemical composition and state of NCDs. The C, N, O elements can be observed in the XPS full spectrum (Fig. 2 a). In the high-resolution spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 2b), the peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV and 288.7 eV are attributed to CC, C–O and CO bonds, respectively [21]. The N 1s spectrum was deconvoluted to two peaks, which were assigned to C–NC (398.6 eV) and N–C3 groups (399.6 eV), respectively (Fig. 2c), indicating that N atoms were successfully doped into carbon quantum dots [32]. The O 1s spectrum in Fig. 2d contains CO (531.8 eV) and C–O–C (533.6 eV) bonds [20]. The above proves that the surface of NCDs possesses a variety of chemical functional groups, which is consistent with the FTIR results. To explore the effect of N doping on the light absorption ability of carbon quantum dots, the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs and NCDs were analyzed (Fig. 2e). Compared with CDs, NCDs exhibited two distinct absorption peaks at 295 nm and 340 nm, which were ascribed to the π-π∗ transition of CC and the n-π∗ transition of CO, respectively [33]. Moreover, the stronger UV light absorption intensity of NCDs in the range of 300–400 nm suggested that the doping of N element enhanced the light absorption ability. The optical properties of NCDs were further probed by investigating up-converted PL spectra at different excitation wavelengths (500 nm–950 nm) [34]. It can be clearly seen that NCDs can absorb long wavelength light (500–950 nm) and then emit photoluminescence at shorter wavelengths (400–550 nm). This implies that NCDs can convert visible and near-infrared light into shorter wavelengths light (400–550 nm) to excite g-C3N4, thereby promoting the light absorption ability of g-C3N4. Therefore, NCDs can serve as an energy transfer element in the design of photocatalysts.

The morphology and microstructure of CN and NCDs(0.5)/CN were observed by SEM and TEM images. As described in Fig. 3 , the CN after secondary calcination presented nanosheet structure (Fig. 3a). After loading NCDs, the nanosheets morphology of NCDs(0.5)/CN did not change significantly (Fig. 3b and c). According to the AFM image and the corresponding height profiles (Fig. 3d–f), the thickness of NCDs(0.5)/CN nanosheet is about 8 nm, which is considered as an ultra-thin nanolayer. This ultra-thin nanolayer structure is beneficial to shorten the transport distance of photogenerated charges and improve the separation efficiency of carriers [1]. Note that evident black spots with a size of about 3 nm can be observed on the surface of the nanosheets (Fig. 3g and h). Its lattice spacing is 0.21 nm (Fig. 3i), which is consistent with that of NCDs in Fig. 1b, representing the (100) crystal plane of graphite carbon. This indicates that N-CQDs were successfully coupled to CN nanosheets.

The XRD patterns of CN and NCDs(x)/CN were manifested in Fig. 4 a. The peaks at 13.2° and 27.9° correspond to the (100) and (002) planes of g-C3N4, respectively, representing the in-plane structures of tri-s-triazine units and interlayer stacking of conjugated aromatic structures [26,35]. Compared with CN, the diffraction peak of NCDs(x)/CN at (002) shifted to the lower angle with the increase of NCDs loading amount, implying that the modification of NCDs enlarged the interlayer spacing of CN. For the FTIR spectra of CN and NCDs(x)/CN (Fig. 4b), the bands at 3050−3350 cm−1 originated from the stretching vibrations of –OH and –NH bonds. The peaks at 1190−1700 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of the CN heterocycles, while the band at 814 cm−1 corresponds to the vibrational modes of triazine units [36]. The loading of NCDs did not alter the overall structure of CN nanosheets. For the sake of exploring the effect of NCDs on the surface state of CN, the XPS spectra NCDs(0.5)/CN and CN were compared (Fig. 4c and d). The N 1s spectra were fitted into three peaks, which were derived from C–NC (398.6 eV), N–C3 (400.1 eV), and C-NHx (401.2 eV), respectively [37,38]. The C 1s spectra were divided into three peaks: CC (284.8 eV), C-NHx (286.1 eV), and N–CN (288.4 eV) [17]. Compared with the XPS spectra of CN, the peaks of N 1s spectra in NCDs(0.5)/CN shifted to higher binding energy, while the peaks of C 1s spectra shifted to lower binding energy, implying the existence of interfacial interaction between NCDs and CN [39]. Moreover, the C/N ratio of CN was 0.79, while that of NCDs(0.5)/CN increased to 1.07 (Table S1), which also proved that NCDs were modified on CN. The area ratios of the C and N atoms in different chemical states were provided in Table S2 and Table S3. Note that the area ratio of CC peak in NCDs(0.5)/CN is higher than that of CN, indicating that the increase of surface C content is caused by the loading of NCDs.

The light absorption properties of CN and NCDs(x)/CN were analyzed by DRS spectra. As displayed in Fig. 5 a, the NCDs(x)/CN appeared stronger absorption intensity and wider absorption range than CN in the UV–visible region, suggesting that the introduction of NCDs improved the light utilization rate of catalyst. The (αhν)2 versus hν curves show that the energy gaps (E
g ) of CN and NCDs(0.5)/CN are approximately 2.69 eV and 2.55 eV, respectively (Fig. 5b). Furthermore, their valence band (E
VB ) values are 1.63 eV and 1.88 eV, respectively (Fig. 5c). According to the formula
E
C B
=
E
V B
−
E g
, the schematic diagram of the energy band structure was presented in Fig. 5d. The results manifested that the modification of NCDs on the surface of CN narrowed the band gap and expanded the light absorption range, which is conducive to boosting the photocatalytic performance.

A series of photoelectrochemical tests were carried out to investigate the separation and transfer behavior of carriers. In the PL spectra (Fig. 6 a), the fluorescence intensity of NCDs(x)/CN was significantly decreased compared with that of CN, indicating that the loading of NCDs can effectively inhibit the recombination of electrons and holes [40]. NCDs(x)/CN displayed a longer fluorescence lifetime than CN (Fig. 6b, Table S4), meaning that the modification of NCDs favors carriers transport, giving carriers more opportunities to participate in photocatalytic reaction [41]. Furthermore, the higher photocurrent response and the smaller EIS arc radius in NCDs(0.5)/CN also proved that it has higher photogenerated carrier separation rate and smaller migration resistance (Fig. 6c and d) [42,43]. The photoelectrochemical tests results demonstrate that the decoration of NCDs on CN can restrain the recombination of electrons-holes and promote the separation and transport of carriers.

The hydrogen production of photocatalysts includes three processes: the generation of electron-hole pairs by photoexcitation, the migration of electrons, and the reaction on the surface of the catalyst. Based on the above investigation, a possible photocatalytic mechanism was proposed (Fig. 9 ). As far as light absorption is concerned, the reasons for improving light utilization involve two aspects. On the one hand, the band gap of NCDs(0.5)/CN is narrowed due to the modification of NCDs, which can absorb visible light with wavelength less than 480 nm to generate electrons and holes (1). On the other hand, long-wavelength light (≥500 nm) can be absorbed by NCDs and converted into shorter-wavelength light (400–500 nm) to excite CN. Owing to the up-conversion effect of NCDs, NCDs(0.5)/CN with enhanced light absorption can stimulate more electrons and holes under light irradiation (2). In terms of carrier separation, the ultrathin structure of CN nanosheets enables the electrons on the conduction band to migrate rapidly to the surface, reducing the chance of electron and hole recombination. More importantly, NCDs loaded on the surface of CN can also act as electron acceptors to easily capture electrons from CN nanosheets (3), thereby further promoting electron-hole separation and surface catalytic reactions. As a result, NCDs/CN composite photocatalyst exhibited enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production performance.

In summary, nitrogen-doped carbon dots modified ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheets composite photocatalysts were prepared by secondary calcination method. A small amount of NCDs-anchored g-C3N4 possesses excellent photocatalytic hydrogen production performance. The hydrogen production rate of g-C3N4 loaded with 0.5 wt% NCDs reaches 5.21 mmol g−1 h−1. Moreover, it also exhibited outstanding cyclic stability. The enhanced photocatalytic mechanism of NCDs(x)/CN composites was analyzed. On the one hand, the 2D ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheets exfoliated by the secondary calcination method are favorable for the carrier migration. On the other hand, the modification of NCDs promotes light absorption and electron transfer of NCDs(x)/CN composites. The enhanced light absorption is attributed to the up-conversion effect of NCDs and the narrow band gap caused by the modification of NCDs. Then, NCDs with excellent optical properties can also serve as electron acceptors, accepting electrons from g-C3N4, thus inhibiting electron-hole recombination and promoting charge separation. Our work may provide a simple and versatile method for the construction of novel carbon dots material combined with g-C3N4 to enhance their photocatalytic activity.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by projects of Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province, China (LH2021B009).
